evidence_quotes.quote: they require a connection to be made with a publisher/developer owned central server, before the game can begin
EULAs typically specify that access may be terminated 'at any time' which is not an acceptable notice

paragraphs.text: Since this act is intended to curb or halt practices within the digital space that are consumer-unfriendly, I would like to see so-called "Live Service Games" added as a point of concern. What I mean by this is games that are designed such that they require a connection to be made with a publisher/developer owned central server, before the game can begin, on each startup of the program. These are games that have been purchased by consumers as goods, yet if the central server is disabled, either by the publisher/developer intentions, or even accidentally, the functionality of the game can be compromised, anywhere from certain features being disabled to a complete non-functional product. This is not purely hypothetical: see, for instance, The Crew or Concord for real examples. While certain features are impractical or impossible to maintain without a central server, it should not be a permitted practice for a publisher/developer to disable a game purchased by a consumer. If the central server is to be shut down for cost-concerns, a functional version (preferably fully, but even minimally functional would be acceptable in cases where the game architecture prohibits anything else) should be provided to consumers who purchased the product as a good. As it stands, a lack of adequate notice of product expiry date on purchasing the game (right now EULAs typically specify that access may be terminated 'at any time' which is not an acceptable notice: if it were any other type of good, such as a computer or automobile, this would be viewed as an overreach of a EULA's terms) or some form of end-of-life plan is a practice I would like to see ended, along with those mentioned in this initiative. The shape of this exact legislation would require some form of exemption or transitional allowances for current or in-development games, and the exact definition of a minimally functional product would have to be somewhat flexible to account for many different scenarios. But in general, I want legislation that will in some way prohibit the remote intentional disabling (for any reason) of the functionality of games. This is in accordance with long-standing precedence, where games have long been purchased with the understanding that they are owned in perpetuity, with the only limiter being hardware limitations and physical deterioration of the medium they are stored on.
